findings.description: Path traversal via cookie value used directly as filename in getCookieFile() - attacker can craft a cookie with traversal sequences to read arbitrary files
findings.code_snippet: File cookieFile = new File( cookieDir, uid );
findings.reasoning: The `uid` value comes directly from the HTTP cookie via `HttpUtil.retrieveCookieValue()` → `getLoginCookie()` → passed to `getCookieFile(engine, uid)`. At line 213, this tainted `uid` is used directly as the filename argument to `new File(cookieDir, uid)` without ANY sanitization or path traversal validation. An attacker can set the cookie to `../../etc/passwd` or similar traversal sequences. The `HttpUtil.retrieveCookieValue()` method only strips surrounding quotes but does NOT filter `../` sequences. The resulting File object is then used for reading (line 124 - FileInputStream) and deleting (clearLoginCookie line 305). This allows arbitrary file read (to infer file existence/contents used for authentication bypass) and arbitrary file deletion.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP cookie → HttpUtil.retrieveCookieValue() [line 76/83] → CookieAuthenticationLoginModule.getLoginCookie() [line 225/227] → login() uid [line 111/116] → getCookieFile(engine, uid) [line 175] → new File(cookieDir, uid) [line 213] → FileInputStream(cookieFile) [line 124]
evidence.notes: The cookie dir is something like /opt/jspwiki/work/cookies/. Setting cookie to '../../../etc/passwd' would resolve to /opt/jspwiki/work/etc/passwd. The username read from any accessible file would be used as the authenticated login name - effectively authentication bypass + arbitrary file read.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sets JSPWiki persistent login cookie to path traversal value to read arbitrary files or perform file deletion on the server
exploit_payload.prerequisites: JSPWiki instance with cookie-based authentication enabled (CookieAssertionLoginModule or CookieAuthenticationLoginModule active)
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal enabling arbitrary file read and file deletion

findings.description: Path traversal via cookie value flows to FileInputStream - cookie UID used as filename without sanitization, allowing arbitrary file read
findings.code_snippet: in = new BufferedReader( new InputStreamReader( new FileInputStream( cookieFile ), "UTF-8" ) );
findings.reasoning: The `cookieFile` object at line 124 is constructed from the raw HTTP cookie value via `getCookieFile(engine, uid)` where `uid` comes from `HttpUtil.retrieveCookieValue()`. No path canonicalization or containment check is performed. The `uid` cookie value flows through: retrieveCookieValue() → getLoginCookie() → login() → getCookieFile() → new File(cookieDir, uid) [line 213] → returned as cookieFile → FileInputStream(cookieFile) at line 124. An attacker crafting a cookie with `../../etc/passwd` would have that file's content read and used as the username for authentication.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Cookie value -> HttpUtil.retrieveCookieValue [line 76,83] -> CookieAuthLoginModule.getLoginCookie [line 225,227] -> uid variable [line 111,116] -> getCookieFile(engine, uid) [line 175] -> new File(cookieDir, uid) [line 213/214] -> FileInputStream(cookieFile) [line 124]

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sets cookie to path traversal value, server reads arbitrary file content and uses it as authenticated username
exploit_payload.prerequisites: JSPWiki with cookie authentication enabled; attacker can set HTTP cookies

findings.description: Path traversal in BasicAttachmentProvider.findAttachmentDir() - attachment filename/parent name from HTTP request flows to File constructor without path containment check
findings.reasoning: The attachment object at line 210 is constructed from HTTP request parameters (attachment name and parent page name). The `att.getParentName()` and `att.getFileName()` values originate from HTTP request parameters (via DefaultURLConstructor, CommandResolver, AttachmentManager). While `mangleName()` applies URL-encoding via `TextUtil.urlEncodeUTF8()`, the `findPageDir()` function uses `mangleName(wikipage)` which URL-encodes the page name, but this is NOT the same as preventing path traversal. The encoded value is passed directly to `new File(m_storageDir, mangleName(wikipage)+DIR_EXTENSION)` without a canonical path containment check. An attacker could potentially supply a page name that after encoding still navigates directories, or use the attachment filename (which is only URL-encoded, not canonicalized) to escape the storage directory.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request (DefaultURLConstructor line 249/267 or WikiContext) -> CommandResolver -> AttachmentManager -> BasicAttachmentProvider.getAttachmentInfo/putAttachmentData -> findAttachmentDir(att) -> new File(findPageDir(att.getParentName()), mangleName(att.getFileName())) [line 210]
evidence.notes: URL encoding (%2F for /) does encode forward slashes but on some systems/configurations URL-decoded values could still traverse. More critically, no canonical path check (getCanonicalPath + startsWith) is performed against m_storageDir.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies crafted attachment page/file name to escape attachment storage directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Authenticated user with attachment upload permission; URL encoding may mitigate in practice

findings.description: Path traversal in BasicAttachmentProvider.getPageProperties() - attachment-derived File path used in FileInputStream without canonical path containment check
findings.reasoning: The `att` object at line 314/319 originates from HTTP request parameters flowing through the attachment resolution chain. `findAttachmentDir(att)` uses `att.getParentName()` and `att.getFileName()` from HTTP input without canonical path containment validation against the base storage directory. The resulting `propertyFile` path could point outside the intended attachment directory. While URL encoding in `mangleName()` provides partial protection, no `File.getCanonicalPath()` + `String.startsWith()` or `Path.startsWith()` containment check is performed.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request -> CommandResolver -> AttachmentManager -> BasicAttachmentProvider -> getPageProperties(att) -> findAttachmentDir(att) -> new File(findPageDir(att.getParentName()), mangleName(att.getFileName())) -> new File(dir, PROPERTY_FILE) -> FileInputStream [line 319]

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies crafted attachment parent/filename to read property files from outside the attachment storage directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Authenticated user with view/download attachment permission
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal in attachment property file read

findings.description: Path traversal in BasicAttachmentProvider.findFile() - attachment-derived File used in file lookup without canonical path containment check against storage directory
findings.reasoning: In `findFile()`, the `ext` value is derived from `att.getFileName()` which comes from HTTP request parameters (attachment name). The file extension is extracted from the HTTP-controlled filename and used directly in `new File(dir, version+"."+ext)`. The `dir` parameter itself is already tainted (from `findAttachmentDir(att)`) as established in previous findings. No canonical path containment check is performed on the resulting File `f` against the base storage directory. An attacker could potentially craft the filename extension to include path separators or other traversal sequences that aren't filtered by URL encoding of the full filename.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request -> AttachmentManager -> BasicAttachmentProvider.getAttachmentInfo/getAttachmentData -> findFile(dir, att) -> getFileExtension(att.getFileName()) -> new File(dir, version+'.'+ext) [line 414]

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts attachment filename with path traversal in extension to access files outside storage directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Authenticated user with attachment access; depends on how getFileExtension handles the input
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal in attachment file access

findings.description: Path traversal in VersioningFileProvider.getPageInfo() - HTTP-supplied page name used in File constructor to access versioned page files without canonical path containment check
findings.code_snippet: File file = new File( dir, version+FILE_EXT );
findings.reasoning: The `page` parameter in `getPageInfo(String page, int version)` originates from HTTP request input via CommandResolver → DefaultPageManager → VersioningFileProvider. The `page` name is passed to `findOldPageDir(page)` which constructs a File path. While page names may be URL-encoded via `mangleName()` in some paths, the resulting directory `dir` is used in `new File(dir, version+FILE_EXT)` at line 585. The full chain from HTTP input to filesystem operation lacks a canonical path containment check (no `getCanonicalPath()` + `startsWith(baseDir)` pattern). An attacker supplying a page name with traversal sequences could potentially access files outside the versioned page storage directory.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request -> CommandResolver.findCommand [line 483] -> WikiEngine.getPage [line 1854/1858] -> DefaultPageManager.getVersionHistory [line 295/303] -> VersioningFileProvider.getPageInfo [line 541] -> findOldPageDir(page) -> new File(dir, version+FILE_EXT) [line 585]

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies crafted page name to have VersioningFileProvider access files outside the page storage directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Page name encoding by mangleName() may mitigate; depends on whether URL encoding is applied before this point
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal in versioned page file access

findings.description: Path traversal in CookieAuthenticationLoginModule.clearLoginCookie() - cookie value used as filename in File.delete() without sanitization, enabling arbitrary file deletion
findings.reasoning: In `clearLoginCookie()`, the `uid` value is retrieved directly from the HTTP cookie via `getLoginCookie(request)` → `HttpUtil.retrieveCookieValue()`. This tainted value is passed to `getCookieFile(engine, uid)` which constructs `new File(cookieDir, uid)` at line 213. The resulting File `cf` is then deleted with `cf.delete()`. No sanitization or path containment check is performed. An attacker who can trigger cookie logout (e.g., by visiting the logout page) can set their cookie to `../../conf/jspwiki.properties` or any other file accessible to the web server process, causing that file to be deleted. This is a reliable arbitrary file deletion vulnerability.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP Cookie -> HttpUtil.retrieveCookieValue() -> getLoginCookie() -> clearLoginCookie uid -> getCookieFile(engine, uid) -> new File(cookieDir, uid) -> File.delete()

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sets JSPWiki login cookie to path traversal value then triggers logout to delete arbitrary files on the server
exploit_payload.prerequisites: JSPWiki with cookie authentication enabled; attacker needs access to logout endpoint

findings.description: Path traversal in BasicAttachmentProvider.putAttachmentData() - HTTP-supplied attachment name/parent page used in FileOutputStream without canonical path containment check, enabling arbitrary file write
findings.reasoning: In `putAttachmentData()`, `attDir` is obtained from `findAttachmentDir(att)` where `att.getParentName()` and `att.getFileName()` both originate from HTTP request parameters. The `attDir` (and thus the base of `newfile`) is not validated against `m_storageDir` using canonical paths. If an attacker can supply a page name or attachment name that survives URL-encoding to contain traversal sequences, the `FileOutputStream` write at line 362 could write to an arbitrary location on the filesystem. This is especially dangerous as it allows writing attacker-controlled content (the attachment data) to an arbitrary file path accessible by the web server process.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request (AttachmentServlet.doPut/doPost) -> AttachmentManager.storeAttachment -> BasicAttachmentProvider.putAttachmentData -> findAttachmentDir(att) -> new File(attDir, version+ext) -> FileOutputStream(newfile) writes HTTP body data

exploit_payload.scenario: Authenticated attacker with attachment upload permission uploads malicious file to arbitrary path via path traversal in page name, enabling remote code execution
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Authenticated user with attachment upload permission; URL encoding in mangleName() may prevent exploitation in practice
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary file write potentially enabling remote code execution

findings.description: Path traversal in BasicAttachmentProvider.findFile() - attachment-derived directory path used in File constructor at line 420 without canonical path containment check
findings.code_snippet: File fOld = new File( dir, version+"." );
findings.reasoning: The `dir` parameter in `findFile()` is the result of `findAttachmentDir(att)` which uses HTTP-supplied attachment name/parent page without canonical path containment validation. At line 420, `new File(dir, version+".")` constructs another File path without checking that the resulting path stays within the base storage directory. While the `version` integer is not attacker-controlled (it's derived from `att.getVersion()` which is parsed from the request), the `dir` base path itself may be outside the intended storage directory if the attachment name/parent contained traversal sequences that survived URL-encoding.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request -> AttachmentManager -> BasicAttachmentProvider -> findAttachmentDir(att) -> dir -> findFile(dir, att) -> new File(dir, version+'.') [line 420]

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker reads arbitrary files from server by crafting attachment requests with traversal page names causing findFile to resolve paths outside storage directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Authenticated user with attachment access; URL encoding mitigation applies
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal in attachment file read

findings.description: Path traversal in CookieAuthenticationLoginModule.getCookieFile() - the File constructor at line 177 uses the work directory base but returns an unsanitized File object that is used for read/write/delete operations throughout the class
findings.code_snippet: File cookieDir = new File( engine.getWorkDir(), COOKIE_DIR );
findings.reasoning: The `getCookieFile()` method at line 177 creates the cookie directory using `engine.getWorkDir()` and `COOKIE_DIR` constant - this part is safe. However, the method at line 213 creates `new File(cookieDir, uid)` where `uid` is the unsanitized cookie value. The method performs no validation that the returned File stays within `cookieDir`. No call to `getCanonicalPath()` with a `startsWith(cookieDir.getCanonicalPath())` check is present. The method is the root cause enabling path traversal in login (line 124), setLoginCookie (line 262 FileOutputStream), and clearLoginCookie (line 305 File.delete).

evidence.dataflow_analysis: getCookieFile is called from: login() with uid from cookie -> FileInputStream read; setLoginCookie() with uuid (safe); clearLoginCookie() with uid from cookie -> File.delete()

exploit_payload.scenario: getCookieFile() is the root of multiple path traversal vulnerabilities - cookie value used as filename enables arbitrary file read, write and delete
exploit_payload.prerequisites: JSPWiki cookie authentication enabled; attacker can control HTTP cookie value
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal root cause - arbitrary file read/write/delete

findings.description: Cookie UID value from HTTP request used directly as filename without path traversal validation in CookieAuthenticationLoginModule
findings.code_snippet: File cookieFile = new File( cookieDir, uid );
findings.reasoning: The `uid` value is read directly from an HTTP cookie via `getLoginCookie(request)` which calls `HttpUtil.retrieveCookieValue()`. This value is then used without validation in `new File(cookieDir, uid)`. An attacker can set their login cookie to a path traversal sequence like `../../../etc/passwd` to read arbitrary files on the filesystem. The `setLoginCookie()` method generates UUID values for new cookies but an attacker can craft their own cookie value. There is no validation that `uid` does not contain path traversal sequences (e.g., `../`), nor is there a canonical path check against the `cookieDir`. The file path at line 213 is constructed with the user-supplied cookie value: `File cookieFile = new File( cookieDir, uid );` and then at line 116 this file's contents are read: `String username = FileUtil.readContents( in );` - allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP cookie value -> getLoginCookie() -> getCookieFile() -> new File(cookieDir, uid) [line 213] -> FileInputStream -> read file contents as username
evidence.notes: No canonicalization or path containment check before file access. mangleName() is not used here. Attacker must know the work directory but can try path traversal sequences.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts an HTTP request with a malicious login cookie value containing path traversal sequences to read arbitrary files from the filesystem, such as /etc/passwd or application configuration files
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must have knowledge of the server's directory structure and the login cookie name (JSPWikiUID). The file must exist and be readable by the servlet process.

findings.description: HTTP 'skin' request parameter flows to getResourceAsStream() via makeFullJSPName() without path traversal validation in TemplateManager.findJSP()
findings.reasoning: The HTTP request parameter `skin` is read in WikiContext (line 837: `template = request.getParameter("skin")`) and stored via `setTemplate()`. This template name is later used in `TemplateManager.findJSP(pageContext, template, name)` which calls `findResource(sContext, template, name)` which calls `makeFullJSPName(template, name)` to produce `/templates/<template>/<name>`. This path is then used in `sContext.getResourceAsStream(path)`. If `template` contains `../`, an attacker can traverse outside the `/templates/` directory to access other resources in the web application, including `WEB-INF/web.xml`, configuration files, or JSP sources. No validation or canonicalization is applied to the `skin` parameter before it is incorporated into the resource path.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP skin param -> WikiContext.setTemplate() -> TemplateManager.findJSP(template) -> makeFullJSPName() -> getResourceAsStream() [lines 162, 182, 186, 209, 214, 229, 290, 312]
evidence.notes: The servlet container may normalize paths before getResourceAsStream(), which could mitigate this. However, some containers allow ../traversal via getResourceAsStream. The resource is only read as a stream, not executed, so this is an information disclosure vulnerability.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies a path traversal sequence in the 'skin' URL parameter to access resources outside the /templates/ directory, potentially reading WEB-INF/web.xml, configuration files, or other sensitive resources
exploit_payload.prerequisites: The application must be accessible and the servlet container must not normalize paths before getResourceAsStream(). Some Servlet containers do normalize these paths.

findings.description: User-controlled servlet name in WikiAjaxDispatcherServlet used in map lookup - not a file path traversal
findings.reasoning: The user-controlled path is used in `ajaxServlets.get(servletAlias)` which is a HashMap lookup against a pre-registered set of servlet names. The servletName from the URL path is extracted via `AjaxUtil.getNextPathPart()` and then used to look up registered servlets. If not found, a `ServletException` is thrown. The `actionName` at line 192 is passed to `servlet.service()` which is an action dispatcher within a registered servlet. This is a map lookup against a whitelist of registered servlets, not a file system path operation. Not a path traversal vulnerability.

evidence.notes: This is a servlet dispatch mechanism using a pre-registered map. The servletName must match a registered servlet or exception is thrown. The actionName is passed to a known servlet implementation. No file operations involved.

findings.description: Attachment filename from HTTP request flows to File construction in BasicAttachmentProvider without sufficient path traversal validation
findings.reasoning: The attachment filename (att.getFileName()) originates from user-controlled HTTP input (page name from request URL or wiki page content). The `mangleName()` method applies `TextUtil.urlEncodeUTF8()` which URL-encodes special characters but does NOT encode dots (`.`). A path traversal sequence like `../evil` would be URL-encoded to `../evil` (unchanged) since neither `.` nor `/` are encoded by standard URL encoding. However, the `findPageDir(att.getParentName())` also applies `mangleName()` (URL encoding) to the parent name, and the full path is constructed as `new File(m_storageDir, encodedParentName+DIR_EXTENSION)`. The question is whether `new File(dir, "../evil-dir")` can escape the parent directory. Multiple code flows from different HTTP input sources (AttachmentServlet, BreadcrumbsTag, VariableManager, CommandResolver) lead to this file construction. Manual review is needed to confirm whether the URL encoding actually prevents traversal and whether the servlet container or wiki engine validates attachment names before they reach the provider. Lines 314, 414, 420 are similar sinks in the same file with the same pattern.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP input (attachment name from URL/request) -> Attachment.getFileName() -> mangleName() -> new File(dir, mangledName) [lines 210, 314, 414, 420]
evidence.notes: URL encoding does encode '/' as '%2F' which would prevent most simple traversal. However dots are not encoded. A filename of '..%2Fevil' passed from HTTP might be decoded somewhere upstream. Need to verify if servlet container URL-decodes the attachment name before it reaches this code, and whether Wiki engine validates attachment names for traversal sequences.

findings.description: HTTP-controlled page name flows to File construction in VersioningFileProvider without verified path traversal protection
findings.code_snippet: File file = new File( dir, version+FILE_EXT );
findings.reasoning: The page name originates from the HTTP request URL parameter (via CommandResolver reading the page parameter from the HTTP request at line 483, flowing through WikiEngine and DefaultPageManager to VersioningFileProvider). The page name is used to construct `findOldPageDir(page)` which builds a File path. While `mangleName()` URL-encodes the page name (encoding `/` as `%2F`), the `version` integer is safely formatted. The underlying `findOldPageDir()` constructs a file path from the page name. The concern is whether the page name URL encoding properly prevents traversal. The code flow is long (7+ hops) and manual review is needed to confirm whether any intermediate layer validates page names for path traversal before they reach the file provider.

evidence.notes: The parent provider (FileSystemProvider) also applies mangleName() which URL-encodes the page name. '/' becomes '%2F'. However dots are not encoded. The severity is lower as version is an integer and the page name mangling should prevent most traversal.
